serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork salami is approximately 3 thin slices (about 30g), commonly used as a topping on pizza, in sandwiches, or on a charcuterie board.

nutrient_source.title: Salami, pork — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salami, pork (CR ID: F06110020). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not reported in the HPB source and are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Salami, pork (FDC ID: 174608), which is an approved secondary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: N/A — Pork salami contains negligible carbohydrates (1.2g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pork salami contains only 1.2g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per Project Veritas v2 rules for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is set to 0.0. No GI value is assigned. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for pork salami, consistent with the negligible-carbohydrate classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (1890mg/100g) — more than three times the 600mg/100g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Pork salami should be avoided entirely by individuals with hypertension; a single 30g serving delivers approximately 567mg sodium, approaching the entire recommended daily limit for sodium-restricted diets, and regular consumption is strongly contraindicated per WHO and AHA sodium guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Ultra-processed red meat with high saturated fat (~13.5g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.3g/100g), independently associated with increased T2D risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of pork salami; as an ultra-processed red meat it is independently associated with increased T2D risk per ADA guidelines, and its high saturated fat content worsens insulin resistance and contributes to adverse glycaemic outcomes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (37g/100g), high saturated fat (~13.5g/100g), trans fat (~0.3g/100g), high sodium (1890mg/100g), and cholesterol (80mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork salami poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its combination of very high sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol; regular consumption is strongly discouraged for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for heart health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile: arachidonic acid from pork, trans fat (~0.3g/100g), high saturated fat (~13.5g/100g), sodium nitrates, and very high sodium (1890mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pork salami entirely; its combination of arachidonic acid, trans fat, saturated fat, and sodium nitrates represents one of the most pro-inflammatory dietary profiles, and sodium nitrates in cured meats are associated with increased inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork salami is a cured and fermented pork sausage of Italian origin, produced by finely grinding or chopping pork and pork fat with salt, spices (typically black pepper, garlic, and fennel), and curing agents including sodium nitrate. The mixture is stuffed into natural or synthetic casings and undergoes a controlled fermentation and air-drying process lasting weeks to months, developing its characteristic firm, dense texture and intensely savoury, slightly tangy flavour. Nutritionally, pork salami is energy-dense at 425 kcal per 100g, with very high fat (37g/100g, of which approximately 13.5g is saturated fat and an estimated 0.3g is trans fat), moderate protein (21.7g/100g), and negligible carbohydrates (1.2g/100g). Its most clinically significant feature is its extremely high sodium content at 1890mg/100g, making it one of the most sodium-dense foods in the processed meat category. Pork salami is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is commonly consumed in thin slices as a pizza topping, in sandwiches, or on charcuterie boards, and is widely available in supermarkets across Singapore and globally.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in salami?
Is pork salami suitable for hypertension?
What is the calorie count of salami?
Can I eat salami if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06110020, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 174608) for pork salami, which is an approved secondary source. All values are internally consistent between sources.
notes: Saturated fat (13.5g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (17.6g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.9g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.3g/100g) are not reported in the HPB source and have been supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Salami, pork (FDC ID: 174608), an approved secondary source. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB source; pork salami contains no plant-based fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.2g/100g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this entry): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin, mineral, and moisture data not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database for this processed meat entry; USDA SR Legacy FDC 174608 does not provide a complete vitamin and mineral panel for this specific salami entry as of the accessed date. Pork salami is not halal-certified as a category; individual branded products may carry halal certification but the generic record cannot assert halal status. This is a Western food of Italian origin; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese multilingual naming requirements apply under G6.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Pork salami record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary, CR ID: F06110020) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (supplementary for fatty acid fractions, FDC ID: 174608), both approved sources. The gi_source.source_match_type has been corrected to 'analogous_estimate' (the closest valid enum value for a not_applicable GI determination) to satisfy the validator constraint. All three required health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with high concern levels and specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible across FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. Alias coverage is comprehensive for an English-language Western food. No multilingual naming requirements apply under G6 for this Italian-origin Western food.
